---
title: "Downloading Land Cover with `geodata`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Downloading Land Cover with `geodata`}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

```{r}
library(LandUsePLN)
```

<!-- WARNING - This vignette is generated by {fusen} from dev/flat_download_landcover_geodata.Rmd: do not edit by hand -->




# Downloading Land Cover with the `geodata` Package

Fortunately, most of the work of interacting with the ESA WorldCover API is handled by the `geodata` package. 
This package provides a convenient interface for downloading and processing geospatial data from
the ESA WorldCover API, including land cover datasets.
```{r}
library(geodata)
```


Using this library is very straightforward — you just provide the
type of land cover you want, and an output dir:
```{r}
landcover_trees <- landcover("trees", path = tempdir())
landcover_trees
```


Clearly this will need a bounding box, since the API
just fetches data for all the globe. To do this,
we can reference `graydon`'s bounding boxes in the
`country-bounding-boxes` package, which we have included as a dependency in our `conda` environment.
We source this function with `reticulate` to get the bounding box for Madagascar:
```{r}
library(reticulate)
library(cli)
library(dplyr)
library(purrr)
library(here)
```


```{r}
reticulate::use_condaenv("/n/home03/ttapera/.local/share/mamba/envs/landuse", required = TRUE)
source_python(here("inst/py/get_bounding_box_for_iso_code.py"))
x <- get_bounding_box_for_iso_code_py("MDG")
box <- x$bbox
box
```


Great, so that is how we will get the bounding box. We
can wrap that in a function:
    
  
  
```{r example-get_bounding_box_for_iso_code}
get_bounding_box_for_iso_code("NPL")
```

  

How does it get used to get a portion of the land use slice?
In the `terra` package, this is done by cropping the `ext`
of the raster:
```{r}
plot(landcover_trees)
```


```{r}
# xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax
ext(landcover_trees)
```


So, to crop the raster, we can simply do:
```{r}
npl_box <- get_bounding_box_for_iso_code("NPL")
cropper <- rast()
xmin(cropper) <- npl_box$xmin
xmax(cropper) <- npl_box$xmax
ymin(cropper) <- npl_box$ymin
ymax(cropper) <- npl_box$ymax
landcover_npl <- crop(landcover_trees, cropper)
plot(landcover_npl)
```


Beautiful! A tiny function to wrap this up will make it modular:
    
  
  
```{r example-crop_landcover_with_box}
crop_landcover_with_box(landcover_raster = landcover_trees, bounding_box = get_bounding_box_for_iso_code("NPL"), plot_invisible = TRUE)
```

  

This seems good. Now, we can wrap the above procedure in a single function
that downloads all of the landcover data for a given country:


```{r examples-download_landcover_geodata}
download_landcover_geodata("NPL", "grassland")
```
